title: Country Risk: Drivers, Measures and Investment Implications - The 2026 Edition!
gist: Damodaran's 2026 country risk update argues that country risk is inescapable and non-diversifiable for both companies and investors, traces cross-country risk variation to political structure, corruption, violence, legal/property rights and climate, and lays out how to convert sovereign ratings and CDS spreads into country equity risk premiums for valuation.

themes.theme: Country risk is inescapable and non-diversifiable

insights.claim: The premise that country risk can be diversified away has been undercut by rising correlation across global equity markets, which spikes precisely during crises when protection matters most.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Investors cannot rely on geographic diversification to neutralize country risk; hedges fail in the crises they are meant to cover, so country risk must be priced explicitly.
insights.quote: The notion that country risk is diversifiable...has been undercut by the increased correlation across global equity markets, and especially so during market crises (which is when you care the most).
insights.locator: The notion that country risk is diversifiable

insights.claim: Because globalization has spread revenue and cost exposure abroad, outside of utilities it is now rare for a company to be entirely domestic on both its revenue and cost sides, so almost every business and investor carries foreign-country risk.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Analysts cannot treat any company as purely domestic; country risk belongs in nearly every valuation regardless of where the firm is headquartered.
insights.locator: For businesses, the exposure to country risk

insights.claim: Technology companies derive a far larger share of revenues from non-domestic markets than manufacturing or service businesses, and foreign exposure tends to increase as companies scale up.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Large-cap tech carries disproportionate embedded country risk, so its cost of equity should reflect global rather than domestic risk premiums.
insights.locator: technology companies, for instance

insights.claim: Home bias in portfolios has declined since the turn of the century, driven by index and mutual funds, lower transaction costs and greater standardization of financial disclosure, though it has not disappeared.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Lower barriers to overseas investing mean more capital is now exposed to country risk it may be under-pricing.
insights.locator: The home bias in portfolios

themes.theme: Four drivers of country risk plus climate

insights.claim: Cross-country variation in business risk can be traced to four factors: political structure (democracy vs authoritarian), corruption, the extent of violence, and the strength of the legal system in enforcing property rights and contracts.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Gives investors a repeatable checklist of measurable drivers to compare countries rather than relying on gut feel about which markets are risky.
insights.locator: I argue that variation in business risk

insights.claim: Only 7.3% of the world's population lived in democracies at the end of 2025, reflecting a decade-long tilt toward authoritarianism per the EIU Democracy Index.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: The global political backdrop for investment is shifting toward authoritarian regimes, changing the character of policy risk investors face.
insights.quote: the tilt towards authoritarianism has increased over the last decade, with only 7.3% of the world's population living in democracies at the end of 2025
insights.locator: Based on these scores

insights.claim: Whether businesses and economies actually perform better under democratic than authoritarian regimes is an open question that the research answers, at best, with a 'maybe.'
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Investors should not automatically discount authoritarian markets on growth grounds; the risk difference is about the shape of policy change, not economic superiority.
insights.locator: there is still an open question

insights.claim: Democracies create more continuous, incremental risk through elections and rule changes, whereas authoritarian regimes offer more policy continuity but expose businesses to larger, more wrenching changes when they do occur.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Risk profile differs by regime type: authoritarian markets carry tail-risk of abrupt discontinuity, which valuation and scenario analysis should reflect.
insights.quote: democratic regimes create more continuous risk for businesses...than authoritarian regimes, where governments can promise more continuity in policy, but when change does come to the latter, it is more likely to be large and wrenching
insights.locator: From a risk perspective

insights.claim: Corruption operates as a hidden tax on business, with Northern Europe scoring least corrupt, followed by Canada, the US and Australia, while large parts of Africa show high corruption and Latin America and much of Asia fall in the middle.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Corruption maps onto a regional risk gradient investors can use to adjust required returns and expected friction costs.
insights.locator: Corruption is a fact of life

insights.claim: The Russia-Ukraine war has created a large zone of violence across Eastern Europe and Russia, and gun violence is a measurable drag on the United States' peace score.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Even developed markets like the US carry violence-related risk drags, and active conflict zones warrant elevated risk premiums.
insights.quote: The Russia-Ukraine war has created a huge area of violence across Eastern Europe and Russia, and exposure to gun violence creates a drag on the United States.
insights.locator: Canada, Australia, Japan and much of Europe

insights.claim: Weak or capricious legal systems that fail to protect property rights and enforce contracts impose costs and risks on businesses, with Latin America, Africa and Asia lagging Europe, Australia and much of North America.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Legal-system quality is a durable regional discriminator of country risk that should feed directly into required returns for operations in weak-rule-of-law markets.
insights.locator: Businesses are dependent on the legal system

insights.claim: Climate risk has not become a major country-risk differentiator because no part of the globe is unaffected and its aggregate effect on corporate bottom lines is not yet big enough to change the discussion.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Investors should treat climate as a company-specific rather than cross-country risk factor for now, watching for the point where it materially hits earnings.
insights.quote: there is no part of the globe that is unaffected, making it less of a differentiator across countries on the risk dimension
insights.locator: There are two reasons why climate risk

insights.claim: Sovereign debt defaults soared in the 1980s and 1990s but have been lower this century, with a shift from loan defaults (usually to banks) toward bond-market defaults.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Bondholders, not banks, now bear more sovereign default losses, changing who is exposed when a country restructures.
insights.locator: Debt defaults, which soared in the 1980s

insights.claim: A non-trivial share of annual sovereign defaults are local-currency defaults, indicating some governments view default as cheaper than the inflation cost of printing currency to repay debt.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Local-currency sovereign debt is not risk-free; investors should not assume a government will always inflate rather than default on its own-currency obligations.
insights.quote: a non-trivial portion of sovereign defaults in each year are local currency defaults
insights.locator: It is also worth noting

insights.claim: Ratings agencies (S&P, Moody's, Fitch) mostly agree on sovereign default assessments and do a reasonably good job despite bias critiques, but they are often slow to act when conditions change.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Sovereign ratings are a usable baseline but lag reality, so investors should supplement them with market-based signals during fast-moving events.
insights.quote: they are often slow to act, when confronted with change
insights.locator: The most widely accessible measures

insights.claim: Sovereign CDS spreads offer a market-based measure of default risk but are available for only 84 countries, leaving swaths of Central and North Africa and frontier markets uncovered.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Market-based default measures have blind spots; the riskiest frontier markets are exactly where CDS data is missing and estimates must be improvised.
insights.locator: Note that sovereign CDS spreads are available

insights.claim: Country risk scores from PRS and the EIU are hard to use because scoring conventions are idiosyncratic and inverted between services, factors and weights differ, and the scores don't convert into cash-flow or discount-rate inputs.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Off-the-shelf composite risk scores can't be dropped into a valuation; analysts need a translation into equity risk premiums.
insights.locator: The table illustrates three problems

insights.claim: PRS's composite risk methodology ranks the United States as riskier than Ghana, illustrating how divergent factor choices and weightings can produce confounding results.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: A vivid warning to scrutinize the methodology behind any country risk score before trusting its relative rankings.
insights.quote: PRS, for instance, ranks the United States as riskier than Ghana, on a composite risk basis
insights.locator: The second is that each service

themes.theme: Estimating equity and country risk premiums

insights.claim: With the S&P 500 at 7499.36 on July 1, 2026, the implied US equity risk premium was 4.42%, and after netting out the 0.22% default spread for the Aa1 rating the mature-market premium was 4.20%.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Provides the concrete base ERP anchor for July 2026 valuations built off a mature-market premium.
insights.quote: the implied equity risk premium for the United States is 4.42%, and netting out the default spread of 0.22% for the Aa1 rating yields a mature market premium of 4.20%
insights.locator: As you can see, with the S&P 500 at 7499.36

insights.claim: Moody's downgrade of the US from Aaa to Aa1 forced Damodaran to abandon using the raw S&P 500 implied ERP as the mature-market base and instead subtract the US default spread from it.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: The US is no longer treated as a pure risk-free mature market; every downstream valuation now carries a small US default adjustment.
insights.quote: The Moody's downgrade for the US, from Aaa to Aa1, has thrown a wrench into that approach, requiring adaptation.
insights.locator: Until 2025, I estimated the mature market premium

insights.claim: Country risk premiums are built by converting sovereign ratings into default spreads and scaling them up by a relative equity/bond risk measure of 1.55 in July 2026, derived from emerging-market equity versus government-bond ETF volatility.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Gives the mechanical recipe for scaling sovereign default spreads into equity risk premiums investors can plug into cost-of-equity estimates.
insights.locator: To estimate country risk premiums

insights.claim: For roughly two dozen countries without sovereign ratings, Damodaran assigns risk premiums by matching their political risk scores to rated countries with similar scores.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Shows how to derive usable premiums for data-poor frontier markets, while flagging these estimates as more makeshift and uncertain.
insights.locator: For the two dozen countries that have no sovereign ratings

insights.claim: Country risk enters a company's valuation through its narrative, and how much depends on where the country sits in the life cycle, so a Venezuelan, Indian or Brazilian company cannot be valued without a strong view of its home country's evolution.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: For firms in risky markets, the country story is inseparable from the valuation, forcing investors to take an explicit macro-political view.
insights.quote: You cannot value a Venezuelan company without taking a strong view about Venezuela, or even an Indian and Brazilian company without an India or Brazil country story underpinning value.
insights.locator: The message from this life cycle view

insights.claim: A company's country risk exposure comes less from where it is incorporated and more from where it actually operates and generates revenue.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Analysts should weight risk premiums by operating and revenue geography, not domicile, when estimating cost of equity.
insights.quote: country risk exposure comes less from where the company is incorporated and more from where it operates
insights.locator: When looking at an individual company

insights.claim: Foreign exposure should be weighted differently by sector: revenues work for consumer product and service companies, production for natural-resource companies, and a mix for manufacturers.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Offers a sector-specific rule for weighting country risk that changes the cost-of-equity build depending on the business model.
insights.locator: revenues work well for consumer product

insights.claim: For a multinational's individual projects, the cost of equity must blend the project's business beta with country risk, and when production and sales sit in different countries the choice depends on where the risk originates.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Capital-budgeting hurdle rates should be tailored per project to the specific business and the country where risk actually resides, using a weighted average when both production and revenue risks apply.
insights.locator: For companies, country risk will also come into play

insights.claim: Currencies are measurement mechanisms that should not command risk premiums on their own, because although they are affected by the same factors that drive country risk they do not determine it.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Investors should not add a separate currency risk premium; currency choice should not change a project's value if cash flows and discount rates are kept in the same currency.
insights.quote: Currencies are measurement mechanisms, and while they may be affected by the same political and economic factors that drive country risk, they don't determine country risk and in my view, should not command risk premiums, on their own.
insights.locator: The reason is simple

insights.claim: A Turkish-lira cost of equity starts from a riskfree rate above 20% while the same project in Euros starts near 3% (the German Euro bond rate), yet the derived value should be identical if cash flows are estimated consistently in each currency.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Reinforces that huge discount-rate gaps across currencies are inflation artifacts, so matching high discount rates with high cash-flow growth avoids currency-driven valuation errors.
insights.locator: When estimating the cost of equity for a Turkish project

insights.claim: A currency's riskfree rate can be built from a base-currency riskfree rate plus the expected inflation differential; if US inflation and riskfree rate are 2.5% and 4% and Brazil's expected inflation is 10.5%, the Brazilian reais riskfree rate should be roughly 12%.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Gives a practical inflation-differential method to estimate riskfree rates where no trustworthy local government bond exists.
insights.quote: if the expected inflation rate and riskfree rate in US dollars are 2.5% and 4% respectively, and the expected inflation rate in Brazil is 10.5%, the riskfree rate in Brazilian reais should be roughly 12%
insights.locator: Put simply, if the expected inflation rate

insights.claim: Currency pegs will hold only if inflation in the pegged currency stays close to inflation in the index currency it is pegged to.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: A signal for spotting unsustainable pegs: diverging inflation between a pegged currency and its anchor foreshadows a break.
insights.quote: currency pegs, when they do exist, will hold only if the inflation in the pegged currency matches or is close to the inflation in the index currency to which it is pegged
insights.locator: The implication of this approach

insights.claim: Errors in expected inflation are largely self-offsetting because they push both the discount rate and the cash-flow growth rate in the same direction.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Reassures analysts that imperfect inflation forecasts won't wreck a valuation as long as they are applied consistently to both numerator and denominator.
insights.locator: the good news is that being wrong on expected inflation
